Title: CPOP1 is a key enzyme required for nodule microenvironment control and successful symbiotic nitrogen fixation in Lotus japonicus

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cofactor for leghemoglobin and other heme-binding proteins; supports microaerobic oxygen buffering/redox homeostasis required for symbiotic nitrogen fixation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Nitrogen-fixing nodules, especially infected cells/infection zone
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Terminal heme-forming branch via protoporphyrinogen IX production
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the oxidative decarboxylation of coproporphyrinogen III to protoporphyrinogen IX in host heme biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes nodule heme accumulation, maintains the microaerobic environment of infected cells, supports leghemoglobin accumulation and successful symbiotic nitrogen fixation; knockout reduces heme and nitrogenase activity and elevates nodule oxygen
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point in nodules; identified as the key component for heme synthesis during symbiotic nitrogen fixation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: LORE1 knockout mutant analysis; complementation/promoter-swap rescue; heme quantification; oxygen microsensor profiling; acetylene reduction assay; qRT-PCR; Western blot for Lb/NifH; confocal localization
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: CPOP family; plastid-targeted coproporphyrinogen III oxidase
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Specifically highly expressed in active nitrogen-fixing nodules; transcripts accumulate during nodule maturation, peak at 10 DPI, then decline and are exhausted in senescent nodules; expression localized to infected cells and nitrogen-fixing zone
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Specifically highly expressed in active nitrogen-fixing nodules; infected-cell specific, driven by promoter region -881 to -740 bp
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Rhizobial inoculation under nitrogen-free Hoagland medium; exogenous KNO3 rescues mutant growth phenotype
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CPOP1 is the most strongly nodule-induced CPO gene in Lotus japonicus and is specifically expressed in infected cells of active nitrogen-fixing nodules. 2. Loss of CPOP1 lowers nodule heme content, causes white nodules, elevates free oxygen in the infection zone, reduces leghemoglobin protein accumulation, and strongly decreases nitrogen fixation activity. 3. CPOP1 promoter-driven CPOP2 or AtCPOP rescues the cpop1 symbiotic defect, indicating that infected-cell specific expression underlies CPOP1 functional specialization.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Knockout causes leaf etiolation, anthocyanin accumulation, dwarfism, reduced biomass, reduced nodule number and nodule weight under nitrogen-limiting conditions
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising target to enhance symbiotic nitrogen fixation by engineering infected-cell specific heme biosynthesis or promoter tuning; deployment should preserve tissue specificity to avoid pleiotropic vegetative defects
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native CPOP1 promoter; infected-cell specific cis-element located in -881 to -740 bp region
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The specialized nitrogen-fixation role depends mainly on promoter evolution rather than unique catalytic function; CPOP1 promoter region -881 to -740 bp is sufficient for infected-cell specific expression and may derive from horizontal sequence transfer.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports nodule function and rhizobia loading/proliferation during symbiosis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Terminal heme-forming branch via protoporphyrinogen IX production
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes conversion of coproporphyrinogen III to protoporphyrinogen IX in soybean heme biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports nodule infected-cell function and bacteroid loading; RNAi knockdown impairs rhizobia loading/proliferation in nodules
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression analysis; native promoter GFP localization; RNAi knockdown in hairy roots; analysis of EMS hypomorphic allele gmcpop (Y192C)
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: CPOP family; plastid-targeted coproporphyrinogen III oxidase
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Higher expression in mature nodules and nodule primordia than in leaves and roots
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher expression in mature nodules and nodule primordia; native promoter drives accumulation chiefly in nodule infection zone
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Rhizobial inoculation under nitrogen-free Hoagland medium
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Soybean contains a single CPOP homolog with high expression in nodules and nodule primordia. 2. Native-promoter GmCPOP localizes to plastids of infected cells in the infection zone. 3. RNAi-mediated downregulation causes more vacuoles and a lower infected-cell/uninfected-cell ratio, indicating impaired rhizobia loading/proliferation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Complete loss of function is seedling-lethal; hypomorphic gmcpop plants are smaller than WT
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for improving soybean nodule function, but strong loss-of-function is deleterious; partial modulation or tissue-specific engineering would be safer
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native GmCPOP promoter; 35S promoter used for RNAi construct
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the paper experimentally analyzes soybean CPOP function as a comparative core gene; gmcpop hypomorphic allele carries Tyr-192 to Cys substitution in a conserved catalytic residue.
